evals.prompt: I'm looking at our Python order processing module at src/services/order_service.py and it's gotten out of hand. The process_order function is like 200 lines long, there's duplicated validation logic between create_order and update_order, and several places are doing raw dict access like order['status'] instead of using typed objects. Can you audit this and suggest what to refactor?
evals.expected_output: Audit with caller counts, identify long method + duplicated code + type safety smells, present tradeoffs, propose specific refactoring steps

assertions.name: caller-count-audit
assertions.description: Runs caller count audit before proposing changes

assertions.name: identifies-long-method
assertions.description: Identifies process_order as a Long Method (>50 lines, multiple responsibilities)

assertions.name: identifies-duplicated-code
assertions.description: Identifies duplicated validation logic between create_order and update_order

assertions.name: identifies-type-safety-issue
assertions.description: Identifies raw dict access as a type safety boundary problem

assertions.name: before-after-diff
assertions.description: Includes at least one BEFORE/AFTER code diff

assertions.name: states-tradeoffs
assertions.description: Explicitly states what got better AND what got worse for each proposed change

assertions.name: surgical-commit-plan
assertions.description: Proposes a sequence of small, separate commits

evals.prompt: Our TypeScript React app has a data transformation module (src/transforms/entry-converter.ts) that's become a mess. There's a bunch of single-use helper functions that only get called once, a giant switch statement where 3 of the 5 branches do basically the same thing (serialize to string then wrap), and the types are defined by duplicating fields instead of using intersections. How should I clean this up?
evals.expected_output: Identify single-caller inlining candidates, collapse duplicate switch branches, derive types via intersection, surgical commit plan

assertions.name: inline-single-callers
assertions.description: Identifies single-use helper functions as inline candidates

assertions.name: collapse-duplicate-branches
assertions.description: Identifies 3-of-5 switch branches doing the same thing and proposes collapsing

assertions.name: type-intersection
assertions.description: Suggests using type intersections/composition instead of duplicating fields

assertions.name: before-after-diff
assertions.description: Includes at least one BEFORE/AFTER code diff

assertions.name: states-tradeoffs
assertions.description: Explicitly states tradeoffs for proposed changes

assertions.name: surgical-commit-plan
assertions.description: Proposes a sequence of small, separate commits

evals.prompt: I'm reviewing a Java service — com.acme.payment.PaymentProcessor — that's become difficult to maintain. It has null checks nested 4 levels deep, validates shipping vs payment vs notification all in one 100-line method, and three different shipping strategies implemented as a long if-else chain. What's the right refactoring approach here?
evals.expected_output: Convert nested conditionals to guard clauses, extract Chain of Responsibility for validation, apply Strategy pattern for shipping, split Large Class by responsibility

assertions.name: guard-clauses
assertions.description: Proposes converting nested null checks to guard clauses / early returns

assertions.name: chain-of-responsibility
assertions.description: Suggests Chain of Responsibility for the multi-step validation logic

assertions.name: strategy-pattern
assertions.description: Suggests Strategy pattern to replace the if-else shipping chain

assertions.name: large-class-split
assertions.description: Identifies Large Class smell and proposes splitting by responsibility

assertions.name: five-axis-scan
assertions.description: NEW SKILL ONLY: Includes a five-axis pre-refactoring assessment scan

assertions.name: severity-labeling
assertions.description: NEW SKILL ONLY: Uses severity labels (Critical/Nit/Optional) for findings

evals.prompt: I refactored our order service and now the unit tests are failing — they were testing internal helper functions that I inlined. Should I update the tests to match the new structure? Also our test file is 800 lines with a lot of duplicated setup code, can we refactor that too while we're at it?
evals.expected_output: Warn that refactoring tests alongside production code breaks the safety net. Tests failing means either behavior changed (not refactoring) or tests were testing implementation details. Advise: revert if behavior changed, or separate test cleanup into its own dedicated pass. Do NOT refactor test file in same pass.

assertions.name: warns-against-test-refactor
assertions.description: NEW SKILL: Explicitly warns NOT to refactor test files alongside production code

assertions.name: diagnoses-test-failure
assertions.description: Diagnoses why tests broke: behavior change vs implementation-detail testing

assertions.name: separate-pass-guidance
assertions.description: Advises test cleanup in a separate, dedicated pass if needed

assertions.name: behavior-preservation-check
assertions.description: Checks whether behavior was actually preserved during the refactor

assertions.name: test-as-spec
assertions.description: NEW SKILL: References tests as the specification of expected behavior

evals.prompt: There's this weird null check in our Python data pipeline at src/pipeline/transform.py line 142 — it guards against a condition that I don't think can ever happen. The code works fine without it when I tested locally. Should I just remove it to simplify the flow?
evals.expected_output: Apply Chesterton's Fence: check git blame for why the null check was added, verify it's truly unreachable before removing. Show the five-axis scan for this specific change. If it's genuinely dead code, flag it and ask before deleting.

assertions.name: chestertons-fence
assertions.description: NEW SKILL: Mentions Chesterton's Fence by name or explains the concept

assertions.name: git-blame-check
assertions.description: NEW SKILL: Suggests checking git blame before removing the null check

assertions.name: five-axis-scan
assertions.description: NEW SKILL: Applies five-axis pre-refactoring scan to this specific change

assertions.name: ask-before-delete
assertions.description: NEW SKILL: Advises asking/confirming before deleting the guard code

assertions.name: dead-code-hygiene
assertions.description: Addresses dead code hygiene: lists what would be removed and asks confirmation

evals.prompt: Our codebase has a utils/helpers.ts file that's become a dumping ground — there's formatDate, formatCurrency, parseDate, parseCurrency, and about 20 other functions. Some are used everywhere, some are used once, and a few appear to be unused. Also some functions take boolean flags like formatDate(date, true, false) which is impossible to understand at call sites. How should I approach cleaning this up?
evals.expected_output: Run caller count audit on every export, apply simplification patterns (boolean flags → options object, generic names → descriptive), identify dead code for deletion, inline single-callers, scope the cleanup to avoid touching unrelated code. Apply Rule of 500 if needed.

assertions.name: caller-count-audit
assertions.description: Runs caller count audit on every export in the helpers file

assertions.name: boolean-flag-pattern
assertions.description: NEW SKILL: Identifies boolean parameter flags and suggests options objects or separate functions

assertions.name: rule-of-500
assertions.description: NEW SKILL: Mentions the Rule of 500 or suggests automation for large changes

assertions.name: dead-code-identification
assertions.description: Identifies unused functions and proposes deletion

assertions.name: scope-discipline
assertions.description: NEW SKILL: Advises scoping cleanup to avoid touching unrelated code

assertions.name: simplification-pattern-table
assertions.description: NEW SKILL: References simplification patterns (structural/naming/redundancy)
